homepage.heroSubtitle: An interactive tour from the modem squeal of the 90s to today's AI era. Open each period through its own model and authentic content.

heroPreview.twoTwentiesLabel: AI tools and a hyper-connected web.

museumReasons.title: The exhibition explains the internet as a story, not just a collection of devices
museumReasons.description: The homepage now gives visitors faster context. Instead of isolated exhibits, it shows why connection speeds, online culture, and everyday digital habits changed over time.

items.title: Each era placed in context
items.description: Every decade presents not only new technologies, but also how they changed communication, work, and the way people spent time online.

items.title: 3D models as entry points
items.description: The models are not only visual decoration. They act as orientation points that lead visitors into the authentic content and story of each era.

items.title: Quick overview, deeper exploration
items.description: The homepage offers a short introduction for first-time orientation while still giving immediate access to the more detailed exhibitions of each period.

footer.title: Digital Museum of the Internet
footer.description: The project combines the visual language of each decade, interactive models, and concise interpretation so that the evolution of the internet stays understandable even for visitors without a deep background in web history.

slide90s.title: 90s
slide90s.label: First steps
slide90s.summary: The internet was slower, but every connection felt like a small adventure. The first websites, chats, and online communities were born.
highlights: First discussion forums and email lists
ICQ, GeoCities, and the Yahoo era

slide2000s.title: 2000s
slide2000s.label: Everyday use
slide2000s.summary: The web got faster with broadband and social networks. The internet moved from enthusiast circles into everyday life.
highlights: Blogs, forums, and early social networks
YouTube, Wikipedia, and early viral content

slide2010s.title: 2010s
slide2010s.label: Mobile revolution
slide2010s.summary: The mobile revolution and social media changed how we communicate and consume content. The internet became omnipresent.
highlights: Rise of smartphones and mobile internet
Boom of social networks and influencers

slidePresent.title: Present
slidePresent.label: AI and metaverse
slidePresent.summary: The internet is faster, smarter, and more connected than ever before. AI, the metaverse, and new technologies are shaping the future of the online world.
highlights: AI and machine learning in everyday life
Increased focus on privacy and security

start.title: Boot the internet museum
start.description: Before entering the exhibition, visitors tune into four digital eras of the internet, from modem noise and CRT screens to today's AI tools and permanently connected web.
start.note: Run RetroNet.exe to enter the exhibition and step through the key moments that shaped the internet.
start.arrowAlt: Arrow pointing at the launch command

nineties.label: First connections
nineties.description: Modem screeches, CRT screens, and a web that is only beginning to speak to a mass audience.

twoThousands.label: The everyday web
twoThousands.description: Broadband, forums, and major platforms turn the internet into an ordinary part of daily life.

twoTens.label: Mobile dominance
twoTens.description: Content moves into our pockets, social networks expand their influence, and streaming becomes standard.

twoTwenties.label: The AI present
twoTwenties.description: A fast, connected, algorithm-driven web creates new possibilities and new questions at the same time.

web90s.underConstruction: 🚧 UNDER CONSTRUCTION 🚧 UNDER CONSTRUCTION 🚧 UNDER CONSTRUCTION 🚧
web90s.infoText: The 1990s were a turning point for technology. Personal computers began appearing in homes, the internet started connecting people across the world, and many devices we still use today began to take shape. Technologies were slower and less powerful than today, but they brought revolutionary possibilities - e-mail, web browsing, and digital communication. This exhibition takes you back to the era when the internet was just beginning to grow. Discover the devices and technologies that shaped the digital world of the 90s.

mouse.title: Computer Mouse
mouse.description: A 90s computer mouse was a basic tool for interacting with a computer. At that time, mice were often equipped with a ball that rotated while moving and transmitted movement data to the computer. These mice usually had two or three buttons, and sometimes a wheel for scrolling. They were essential for navigating graphical user interfaces and allowed users to control their computers more easily.

monitor.title: Monitor
monitor.description: Computer monitors in the 90s were large and heavy CRT screens (cathode-ray tubes). They looked similar to old televisions and could weigh more than 15 kilograms. They had lower resolution than modern displays and sometimes flickered slightly. Even so, they enabled colorful graphical interfaces, games, and the first web pages.

floppy.title: Floppy Disk
floppy.description: The floppy disk (most commonly 3.5-inch) was a common medium in the 90s for storing and transferring data. It had a capacity of 1.44 MB, enough for documents, small programs, or saved game files. It was also used for software installation and file transfer between computers. Although practical, it was sensitive to physical damage and magnetic fields.

javascript.title: The Release of JavaScript
javascript.description: JavaScript was introduced in 1995 at Netscape, and Brendan Eich built its first version in roughly ten days. The language was first called Mocha, then LiveScript, and it was renamed JavaScript largely because Java was extremely popular at the time. Its main purpose was to bring interactivity to web pages, such as click reactions, simple form validation, and changing content without relying only on HTML. In the 90s it laid the groundwork for the modern frontend and became one of the most influential languages on the internet.

hero.title: Welcome to the web of the 2010s
hero.subtitle: This virtual exhibition explores the unique technologies and trends that defined this era.

smartphones.title: Smartphones and the Mobile Revolution
smartphones.description:
- The smartphone became the center of everyday life - from alarm clocks to banking
- 4G mobile internet enabled streaming video, music, and live broadcasts from almost anywhere
- The App Store and Google Play launched an era of custom apps for work, school, and free time
- Built-in cameras turned phones into real-time tools for documenting moments
- Touch controls, notifications, and biometrics set a new standard for user comfort

socialMedia.title: Social Media and Digital Communication
socialMedia.description:
- Social networks transformed the internet from passive reading into nonstop interaction
- Facebook, Instagram, X, and TikTok introduced new ways to communicate, entertain, and express identity
- Stories, reels, and short videos accelerated content pace and shifted user attention
- Influencer marketing reshaped advertising into personality-driven recommendations
- Viral trends could influence music, fashion, politics, and public debate within hours

cloud.title: Cloud Computing and Online Services
cloud.description:
- The cloud moved data storage and computing power from local machines to online infrastructure
- Services like Google Drive, OneDrive, and Dropbox made files accessible from anywhere in seconds
- Web apps replaced part of traditional software and reduced the need for local installations
- Real-time collaboration (documents, chats, spreadsheets) dramatically sped up team workflows
- Synchronization across phone, laptop, and tablet created a seamless digital ecosystem

milestones.title: Milestones of the 2010s
milestones.intro: A quick look at the events that most strongly reshaped how people used the internet during the decade.

2010.title: Instagram and mobile photo culture
2010.description: Photo sharing shifted to phones, and social content started to be created primarily on mobile.

2012.title: Rise of 4G networks
2012.description: Faster mobile internet accelerated video content, real-time maps, and cloud-powered apps.

2016.title: Live streaming boom
2016.description: Live video on social platforms made real-time broadcasting accessible to everyday users and brands.

2019.title: Short videos become the main format
2019.description: Vertical short-form videos started to dominate feeds and changed how people discover new content.

hero.title: ✨ Welcome to Web 2.0!
hero.description: The internet has changed. Users are no longer just readers - they are content creators! Blogs, social networks, and content sharing changed everything.

ipod.title: 🎵 iPod brings music straight to your pocket
ipod.content: Current iPod models let you play thousands of songs in a compact and lightweight design. Thanks to modern displays and intuitive controls, access to music is faster and more convenient than with older portable players from before 2000.

myspaceFacebook.title: 📰 MySpace & Facebook are changing social networks
myspaceFacebook.content: The internet has changed. Users are no longer just readers - they are content creators! Blogs, social networks, and content sharing changed everything.

youtube.title: 📹 YouTube: Video Revolution
youtube.content: From a garage to billions of views. YouTube changed how we consume media. Every minute, 10 hours of video are uploaded!

iphone.title: 📱 iPhone unveiled!
iphone.content: Apple has just introduced a revolutionary device. It is an iPod, a phone, and an internet device all in one!

mouse.title: 🖱️ A new generation of computer mice boosts precision and comfort
mouse.content: Modern computer mice now deliver much higher precision, faster response, and better ergonomics than models from before 2000. Thanks to advanced sensors, wireless technologies, and new materials, both work and gaming are more comfortable and accurate than ever.

monitor.title: 🖥️ New-generation monitors bring sharper visuals and better comfort
monitor.content: Modern computer monitors now offer significantly higher resolution, better colors, and faster refresh rates than models before 2000. Thanks to newer panel types and slimmer designs, both work and media viewing are more pleasant and visuals are much sharper.

flashDrive.title: 💾 Flash drives are changing data storage
flashDrive.content: Current USB flash drives offer much larger capacity and faster data transfer than older portable storage from before 2000. Thanks to their small size and simple USB connection, they make it easy to move files between computers and other devices.

hero.subtitle: From mobile apps to AI, today's internet is everywhere around us. Minimal, fast, and constantly connected.

featureCards.title: Mobile first
featureCards.description: Today's web is designed primarily for mobile devices. Fast loading, clear interfaces, and touch-friendly interactions are the baseline of a good user experience.

featureCards.title: AI and personalization
featureCards.description: The modern web uses artificial intelligence for recommendations, search, and direct user assistance. Chatbots, recommendation systems, and generative models are now part of mainstream services.

featureCards.title: Cloud and edge computing
featureCards.description: Today's web runs in the cloud and increasingly relies on edge infrastructure for lower latency. Serverless services and CDNs help deliver content without long waits.

featureCards.title: Cybersecurity
featureCards.description: As society depends more on internet services, security becomes critical. Data protection, encryption, and secure protocols are essential for user trust and reliable platforms.

featureCards.title: Web3 and decentralization
featureCards.description: The decentralized web promises stronger control over data and digital identity. Blockchain, digital assets, and distributed applications illustrate one possible direction for the next stage of the internet.

items.title: The pandemic accelerates cloud adoption and remote work

headsetExhibit.title: Immersive Headsets and the Spatial Web
headsetExhibit.description: Modern headsets combine VR, AR, and mixed reality into one device, moving the internet from flat pages into interactive 3D spaces. They are used in gaming, design workflows, education, virtual events, and remote collaboration, where people can share the same digital scene in real time. This is one of the defining trends of the 2020s: online experiences feel less like browsing and more like presence.
